🇸🇰 SK

ECB SAFE Survey — Wave 37 · Slovakia

Slovakia · Euro Area · Germany  |  Generated 07 Jul 2026

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Executive Summary

  • Cost-margin squeeze: Labour costs (net 62%) and other costs (net 58%) rose well above EA peers while turnover stayed negative at net -15% against the EA's positive net 6% — a near-21pp divergence — leaving Slovak profits still in decline at net -22% despite a 15pp improvement.
  • Bank loan gap reversed: The Slovak bank loan financing gap swung from +10.5pp to -7.6pp — 11.3pp below the EA's +3.7pp — as need fell sharply and availability turned positive, while trade credit remained the most stressed instrument at +9.8pp.
  • Loan-size conditions tightened: Available loan size shifted from a net 7.9% reporting easing to a net 7.0% reporting tightening — a 14.9pp reversal — and non-interest costs were reported as net tightening by 42.4% of firms, well above the EA's 28.4%, even as bank willingness to lend improved.
  • Investment sentiment weakens: Slovak firms' fixed investment expectations fell sharply to net 11%, a 14.9pp decline from net 26%, far exceeding the EA's marginal 1.3pp easing to net 13%, signalling a growing divergence in investment intent.

Financing Conditions

Slovak interest rate conditions ease, but gap with EA narrows notably

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • Available loan size conditions for Slovak firms (n=48) tightened notably, shifting from a net 7.9% reporting easing in the prior wave to a net 7.0% reporting tightening — a 14.9 pp reversal — while the EA edged marginally less tight at a net 3.7%, suggesting Slovak firms face increasingly constrained access to financing.
  • Slovak firms (n=48) remained the only group reporting net easing on interest rates at a net 8.3%, yet this favourable balance narrowed substantially by 16.9 pp from the prior wave's net 25.2%, converging toward the EA's net tightening of 11.1% (n=1,750), indicating a rapid loss of their previous relative advantage.
  • Non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions) were reported as net tightening by 42.4% of Slovak firms (n=48), well above the EA net tightening of 28.4% (n=1,762) and approaching DE's net tightening of 48.1%, putting additional pressure on firms' cost structures.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak bank loan gap reversed sharply while trade credit stress persisted

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • The Slovak bank loan financing gap reversed from +10.5pp to -7.6pp — turning 11.3pp below the EA gap of +3.7pp — as need fell sharply from +5.4pp to -6.2pp and availability turned positive from -5.1pp to +1.4pp, with falling need as the dominant driver, suggesting a significant easing of financing constraints for Slovak firms.
  • While bank loan conditions moved into net ease, the credit line gap remained positive at +4.8pp (n=256), sustained by persistent need (+3.1pp) and continued availability restraint (-1.7pp); notably, however, this gap was 1.8pp below the EA's +6.6pp, reversing the prior wave's +5.1pp overshoot, indicating some residual pressure on firms reliant on credit lines.
  • Trade credit remained the most stressed instrument for Slovak firms (n=176): the gap narrowed from +16.4pp to +9.8pp — 3.0pp above the EA's +6.8pp — driven almost entirely by availability improving from -6.7pp to +0.3pp, while need edged further up from +9.7pp to +10.1pp, highlighting ongoing liquidity challenges despite slight improvements.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak firms' bank loan applications fell sharply, narrowing the financing gap

Bank Loan Applications and Access Conditions (Q7A/Q7B)

  • The share of Slovak firms that actually applied for bank loans fell from 46.16% (wave 36, n=90) to 23.51% (wave 37, n=147), contrasting with a rise from 30.55% to 35.93% in the EA (n=3,406) — the largest divergence in application behaviour between Slovakia and the euro area in recent waves, suggesting a sharp tightening in credit demand among Slovak firms.
  • The financing gap — the share of firms seeking bank credit but unable to obtain it, combining discouraged non-applicants and rejected applicants as a share of all respondents — narrowed by 3.80 pp to 3.58% in Slovakia (n=147), falling below the EA's 5.02% (n=3,406), which itself widened by 1.48 pp from the prior wave, indicating improved credit access relative to peers.
  • Both gap components eased in Slovakia: the discouragement rate (firms fearing rejection and not applying) declined from 3.33% to 2.34% (n=147), and the rejection rate among applicants fell from 8.78% to 5.31% (n=147), compared with EA rejection rates that rose from 2.73% to 5.00% (n=3,406), reflecting improved sentiment and approval conditions for Slovak firms.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms' bank loan availability expectations improved sharply, converging with EA

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net 1.93% of Slovak firms (n=125) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate in wave 37, improving sharply by 10.69 pp from a net 12.62% expecting deterioration in wave 36 — suggesting reduced headwinds for firms seeking external financing.
  • The SK–EA gap narrowed from 13.44 pp in wave 36 to just 1.48 pp in wave 37, as Slovak expectations converged towards the EA net balance of -0.45% (n=2,688) — the closest alignment in recent waves, indicating improved financing prospects relative to the euro area.
  • EA expectations for bank loans edged marginally in the adverse direction, with the EA net balance moving from +0.82% in wave 36 to -0.45% in wave 37 (Δ −1.27 pp), a slight shift while Slovak firms improved substantially — highlighting divergent trends between the regions.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms increasingly finance working capital and product development

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • Inventory and working capital remained the dominant financing purpose for Slovak firms (n=237), cited by 55.8% — 17.0 pp above the EA average of 38.8% and up 2.6 pp from the previous wave's 53.2%, suggesting a persistent structural reliance on short-term liquidity management.
  • The share of Slovak firms (n=237) financing the development or launch of new products rose from 21.5% to 29.9% (Δ +8.4 pp), the largest single-purpose increase in wave 37, bringing SK 8.1 pp above the EA's 21.8%, indicating a strategic shift toward innovation-driven growth.
  • Refinancing or paying off obligations was cited by 20.6% of Slovak firms (n=236), rising 5.9 pp from the prior wave's 14.7%, while the EA share declined by 2.7 pp to 14.3% — widening the SK–EA gap to 6.3 pp, reflecting heightened debt servicing pressures relative to peers.

Slovak firms report bank willingness to lend turned positive in wave 37

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net 10% of Slovak firms (n=300) reported improved willingness of banks to provide credit in wave 37, turning positive from a net 2% reporting deterioration in the previous wave — surpassing the EA net balance of 6%, suggesting a more favourable financing environment for firms.
  • Slovak firms' assessment of the general economic outlook remained deeply negative at a net 38%, though this improved by 12.9 pp from the prior wave's net 51%; the EA net balance of 21% negative was notably less adverse, highlighting ongoing macroeconomic concerns for firms.
  • Access to public financial support improved by 8.9 pp for Slovak firms (n=328) but remained negative at a net 25%, compared with the EA net balance of 15% negative, indicating a persistent shortfall in perceived public financing support — underscoring continued reliance on alternative financing channels.

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak firm profits improved notably but turnover remains negative while EA turns positive

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net 22% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported declining profits in wave 37, improving by 15 pp from a net 37% in the prior quarter — the largest single-wave improvement across all business situation indicators for Slovakia, though still more adverse than the EA's net 10%, suggesting a significant recovery trend.
  • Slovak turnover remained negative at a net 15% of firms reporting a decline (n=237), easing only marginally by 0.5 pp from the previous quarter, while the EA aggregate turned positive at a net 6% — a divergence of nearly 21 pp between Slovakia and the EA, indicating persistent competitiveness challenges.
  • Labour costs continued to press more heavily on Slovak firms than on EA peers, with a net 62% of Slovak firms (n=237) reporting increases — 12 pp above the EA net of 50% — while other costs rose to a net 58%, up 15 pp from the previous quarter, signalling mounting cost pressures.

Slovak fixed investment expectations fell sharply while EA held steady

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • Slovak firms' fixed investment expectations fell sharply to a net 11% (n=169), a decline of 14.91 pp from the prior wave's net 26% — far exceeding the EA's marginal easing of 1.27 pp to a net 13%, suggesting a growing divergence in investment sentiment between Slovakia and the euro area.
  • Turnover expectations for Slovak firms moderated to a net 21% (n=169), easing 3.28 pp from the previous wave's net 24% — broadly in line with the EA's parallel decline of 4.62 pp to a net 23%, implying synchronized demand softening across regions.
  • Despite the pullback, Slovak firms' turnover outlook remained positive, with net optimism of 21% close to the EA average of net 23%, indicating no broad-based deterioration in demand expectations.

Slovak firms' selling price and input cost expectations rose notably in wave 37

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • Slovak firms expected average selling prices to rise by 4.63% over the next 12 months (n=226), up 1.22 pp from 3.41% in wave 36 — well above the EA's 2.77% (Δ −0.12 pp), pointing to sustained domestic price pressures.
  • Slovak firms anticipate 8.37% non-labour input price growth (n=230, Δ +2.52 pp vs wave 36) alongside 5.49% wage growth (n=233), while a net 61.84% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported labour costs increasing in the current wave — the highest cost-side net balance in the sample — compressing profit margins, which showed a net 22.43% of firms reporting a decline.
  • Slovak firms' near-term inflation expectations rose from 4.34% to 4.79% (n=211, Δ +0.45 pp), diverging further from the EA's 3.36% (Δ −0.04 pp), with employee headcount expectations recovering marginally from +0.06% to +0.63% (n=232) but remaining below the EA's +0.97%.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms see inflation risks skewed to the upside, outpacing EA

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 53% of Slovak firms (n=237) perceived inflation risks as skewed to the upside in wave 37, exceeding the EA net balance of 45% — a gap of 8 pp pointing to greater local price persistence. • Slovak firms' upside inflation risk balance eased by 7 pp from the prior wave's net 60% (n=134), while the EA balance rose moderately by 5 pp from net 41%, narrowing but not closing the SK–EA divergence. • Slovak firms' mean expected selling price increase stood at 4.63% (n=226) in wave 37, well above the EA mean of 2.77% (n=4807), reinforcing the elevated upside risk balance on the inflation outlook.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Skilled staff scarcity tops Slovak business concerns; regulation pressingness rises most

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Availability of skilled staff or experienced managers ranked as the most pressing among the seven tested for Slovak firms (n=237), scoring 6.57/10 — a moderate-to-high level of concern — marginally above the EA average of 6.64/10 and up 0.12 points from wave 30's 6.45/10, underscoring persistent hiring challenges that could constrain growth.
  • Regulation recorded the sharpest rise in pressingness among all seven problems for Slovakia, climbing 1.06 points from 5.00/10 in wave 30 to 6.06/10 in wave 37 (n=233), overtaking the EA's 6.04/10 and moving from a low- to a moderate-priority concern, which may signal rising compliance costs or uncertainty for firms.
  • Costs of production or labour scored 6.34/10 (n=237), placing second in Slovakia's ranking and rising 0.28 points from wave 30's 6.06/10, though remaining modestly below the EA's 6.66/10; access to finance, at 3.72/10 (n=233), continued to register as a low-priority concern for Slovak firms, suggesting limited immediate liquidity pressures despite cost pressures.

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
